message.content:
Build a signup and login page using supabase auth

In signup page (src/app/(auth)/signup/page.tsx):
- Use fields like email, password, handle(@username)
- then do validation like it have 3-20 chars, lowercase letters, numbers, underscores only
- When we submit the form then call supabase signup, then insert a row into profile table via prisma then send a welcome email via Resend
- After signup redirect to /dashboard
- and in signup page use a link to redirect to login page

In Login page (src/app/(auth)/login/page.tsx):
- Use fields like email and password
- When we submit the form then call supabase signin with password
- After login, redirect user to /dashboard
- and in login page also, use a link to redirect to signup page

For API Routes use:
- POST /api/auth/signup: handle signup logic + create profile in DB + send Resend email
- POST /api/auth/login: handle login logic

Now UI requirements are:
- Use shadcn/ui components that needed
- Use clean, minimal and light design with white background
- Don't use dark colors
- Show toast message when user is successfully signup or login
- Show loading state on submit button
- Show error message inline

1 --- 2 title: How to create forms with Server Actions 3 nav_title: Forms 4 description: Learn how to create forms in Next.js with React Server Actions. 5 --- 6 7 React Server Actions are [Server Functions](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-functions) that execute on the server. They can be called in Server and Client Components to handle form submissions. This guide will walk you through how to create forms in Next.js with Server Actions. 8 9 > [!WARNING] 10 > Always verify [authentication and authorization](/docs/app/guides/authentication) inside each Server Action, even if the form is only rendered on an authenticated page. See the [Data Security guide](/docs/app/guides/data-security) for more details. 11 12 ## How it works 13 14 React extends the HTML [`
`](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTML/Element/form) element to allow Server Actions to be invoked with the [`action`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Reference/Elements/form#action) attribute. 15 16 When used in a form, the function automatically receives the [`FormData`](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/FormData/FormData) object. You can then extract the data using the native [`FormData` methods](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/FormData#instance_methods): 17 18 ```tsx filename="app/invoices/page.tsx" switcher 19 import { auth } from '@/lib/auth' 20 21 export default function Page() { 22 async function createInvoice(formData: FormData) { 23 'use server' 24 25 const session = await auth() 26 if (!session?.user) { 27 throw new Error('Unauthorized') 28 } 29 30 const rawFormData = { 31 customerId: formData.get('customerId'), 32 amount: formData.get('amount'), 33 status: formData.get('status'), 34 } 35 36 // mutate data 37 // revalidate the cache 38 } 39 40 return ...
41 } 42 ``` 43 44 ```jsx filename="app/invoices/page.js" switcher 45 import { auth } from '@/lib/auth' 46 47 export default function Page() { 48 async function createInvoice(formData) { 49 'use server' 50 51 const session = await auth() 52 if (!session?.user) { 53 throw new Error('Unauthorized') 54 } 55 56 const rawFormData = { 57 customerId: formData.get('customerId'), 58 amount: formData.get('amount'), 59 status: formData.get('status'), 60 } 61 62 // mutate data 63 // revalidate the cache 64 } 65 66 return
...
67 } 68 ``` 69 70 > **Good to know:** When working with forms that have multiple fields, use JavaScript's [`Object.fromEntries()`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Object/fromEntries). For example: `const rawFormData = Object.fromEntries(formData)`. Note that this object will contain extra properties prefixed with `$ACTION_`. 71 72 ## Passing additional arguments 73 74 Outside of form fields, you can pass additional arguments to a Server Function using the JavaScript [`bind`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Function/bind) method. For example, to pass the `userId` argument to the `updateUser` Server Function: 75 76 ```tsx filename="app/client-component.tsx" highlight={6} switcher 77 'use client' 78 79 import { updateUser } from './actions' 80 81 export function UserProfile({ userId }: { userId: string }) { 82 const updateUserWithId = updateUser.bind(null, userId) 83 84 return ( 85
86 87 88
89 ) 90 } 91 ``` 92 93 ```jsx filename="app/client-component.js" highlight={6} switcher 94 'use client' 95 96 import { updateUser } from './actions' 97 98 export function UserProfile({ userId }) { 99 const updateUserWithId = updateUser.bind(null, userId) 100 101 return ( 102
103 104 105
106 ) 107 } 108 ``` 109 110 The Server Function will receive the `userId` as an additional argument: 111 112 ```ts filename="app/actions.ts" switcher 113 'use server' 114 115 export async function updateUser(userId: string, formData: FormData) {} 116 ``` 117 118 ```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher 119 'use server' 120 121 export async function updateUser(userId, formData) {} 122 ``` 123 124 > **Good to know**: 125 > 126 > - An alternative is to pass arguments as hidden input fields in the form (e.g. ``). However, the value will be part of the rendered HTML and will not be encoded. 127 > - `bind` works in both Server and Client Components and supports progressive enhancement. 128 129 ## Form validation 130 131 Forms can be validated on the client or server. 132 133 - For **client-side validation**, you can use the HTML attributes like `required` and `type="email"` for basic validation. 134 - For **server-side validation**, you can use a library like [zod](https://zod.dev/) to validate the form fields. For example: 135 136 ```tsx filename="app/actions.ts" switcher 137 'use server' 138 139 import { z } from 'zod' 140 141 const schema = z.object({ 142 email: z.string({ 143 invalid_type_error: 'Invalid Email', 144 }), 145 }) 146 147 export default async function createUser(formData: FormData) { 148 const validatedFields = schema.safeParse({ 149 email: formData.get('email'), 150 }) 151 152 // Return early if the form data is invalid 153 if (!validatedFields.success) { 154 return { 155 errors: validatedFields.error.flatten().fieldErrors, 156 } 157 } 158 159 // Mutate data 160 } 161 ``` 162 163 ```jsx filename="app/actions.js" switcher 164 'use server' 165 166 import { z } from 'zod' 167 168 const schema = z.object({ 169 email: z.string({ 170 invalid_type_error: 'Invalid Email', 171 }), 172 }) 173 174 export default async function createUser(formData) { 175 const validatedFields = schema.safeParse({ 176 email: formData.get('email'), 177 }) 178 179 // Return early if the form data is invalid 180 if (!validatedFields.success) { 181 return { 182 errors: validatedFields.error.flatten().fieldErrors, 183 } 184 } 185 186 // Mutate data 187 } 188 ``` 189 190 ## Validation errors 191 192 To display validation errors or messages, turn the component that defines the `

## Route Handlers

Route Handlers allow you to create custom request handlers for a given route using the Web [Request](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Request) and [Response](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Response) APIs.

<Image
  alt="Route.js Special File"
  srcLight="/docs/light/route-special-file.png"
  srcDark="/docs/dark/route-special-file.png"
  width="1600"
  height="444"
/>

> **Good to know**: Route Handlers are only available inside the `app` directory. They are the equivalent of [API Routes](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/api-routes) inside the `pages` directory meaning you **do not** need to use API Routes and Route Handlers together.

### Convention

Route Handlers are defined in a [`route.js|ts` file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) inside the `app` directory:

```ts filename="app/api/route.ts" switcher
export async function GET(request: Request) {}
```

```js filename="app/api/route.js" switcher
export async function GET(request) {}
```

Route Handlers can be nested anywhere inside the `app` directory, similar to `page.js` and `layout.js`. But there **cannot** be a `route.js` file at the same route segment level as `page.js`.

### Supported HTTP Methods

The following [HTTP methods](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTTP/Methods) are supported: `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `PATCH`, `DELETE`, `HEAD`, and `OPTIONS`. If an unsupported method is called, Next.js will return a `405 Method Not Allowed` response.

### Extended `NextRequest` and `NextResponse` APIs

In addition to supporting the native [Request](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Request) and [Response](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Response) APIs, Next.js extends them with [`NextRequest`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/next-request) and [`NextResponse`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/next-response) to provide convenient helpers for advanced use cases.

### Caching

Route Handlers are not cached by default. You can, however, opt into caching for `GET` methods. Other supported HTTP methods are **not** cached. To cache a `GET` method, use a [route config option](/docs/app/guides/caching-without-cache-components#dynamic) such as `export const dynamic = 'force-static'` in your Route Handler file.

```ts filename="app/items/route.ts" switcher
export const dynamic = 'force-static'

export async function GET() {
  const res = await fetch('https://data.mongodb-api.com/...', {
    headers: {
      'Content-Type': 'application/json',
      'API-Key': process.env.DATA_API_KEY,

content.text:
Everything looks good. Here's a summary of what was built:

---

**Files created/modified:**

- `src/app/api/auth/signup/route.ts` — validates handle format + uniqueness via Prisma, calls `supabase.auth.signUp`, creates `User` + `Profile` in Prisma (using Supabase UUID as the Prisma ID), sends welcome email via Resend (non-blocking), and returns session cookies on the response.

- `src/app/api/auth/login/route.ts` — calls `supabase.auth.signInWithPassword` and returns session cookies on the response.

- `src/app/(auth)/signup/page.tsx` — client form with email, `@handle`, password fields. Client-side validation runs first (regex + length), then POSTs to the API. Shows inline field errors, a banner for form-level errors, a loading spinner on the button, and a `toast.success` on success before redirecting to `/dashboard`. Links to `/login`.

- `src/app/(auth)/login/page.tsx` — client form with email + password. Same loading/error pattern, `toast.success("Welcome back!")` on success, redirects to `/dashboard`. Links to `/signup`.

- `src/app/layout.tsx` — added `<Toaster theme="light" position="top-center" richColors />`.

**A note on email confirmation:** If your Supabase project has email confirmation enabled (the default), Supabase won't return a session on signup — the API returns `{ requiresConfirmation: true }` and the page shows a `toast.info` asking the user to check their email instead of redirecting. To get the immediate redirect to `/dashboard`, disable "Confirm email" in your Supabase Auth settings.

# Prisma Client · [![npm version](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/@prisma/client.svg?style=flat)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@prisma/client) [![PRs Welcome](https://img.shields.io/badge/PRs-welcome-brightgreen.svg)](https://github.com/prisma/prisma/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) [![GitHub license](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-Apache%202-blue)](https://github.com/prisma/prisma/blob/main/LICENSE) [![Discord](https://img.shields.io/discord/937751382725886062?label=Discord)](https://pris.ly/discord)

Prisma Client JS is an **auto-generated query builder** that enables **type-safe** database access and **reduces boilerplate**. You can use it as an alternative to traditional ORMs such as Sequelize, TypeORM or SQL query builders like knex.js.

It is part of the [Prisma](https://www.prisma.io/) ecosystem. Prisma provides database tools for data access, declarative data modeling, schema migrations and visual data management. Learn more in the main [`prisma`](https://github.com/prisma/prisma/) repository or read the [documentation](https://www.prisma.io/docs/).

## Getting started

Follow one of these guides to get started with Prisma Client JS:

- [Quickstart](https://www.prisma.io/docs/getting-started/prisma-orm/quickstart/prisma-postgres) (5 min)
- [Getting started with Prisma Migrate](https://www.prisma.io/docs/orm/prisma-migrate/getting-started) (15 min)
- [Add Prisma to an existing project](https://www.prisma.io/docs/getting-started/prisma-orm/add-to-existing-project/prisma-postgres) (15 min)

Alternatively you can explore the ready-to-run [examples](https://github.com/prisma/prisma-examples/) (REST, GraphQL, gRPC, plain JavaScript and TypeScript demos, ...) or watch the [demo videos](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0RhtQgIs-TE&REDACTED&index=1) (1-2 min per video).

## Contributing

Refer to our [contribution guidelines](https://github.com/prisma/prisma/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) and [Code of Conduct for contributors](https://github.com/prisma/prisma/blob/main/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md).

content.text:
Found the issue. In Prisma 7, the default engine changed to `"client"` (WASM-based), which **requires** a driver adapter or Prisma Accelerate. The schema has no `engineType` set so it defaults to `"client"`. The fix is to either use a driver adapter or switch back to the `"library"` (binary) engine.

For a standard Supabase Postgres connection, the driver adapter approach (`@prisma/adapter-pg`) is the correct Prisma 7 path. Let me implement that:

content.text:
Clean. Here's what changed and why:

**Root cause:** Prisma 7 switched the default engine to `"client"` (WASM-based), which requires either a driver adapter or Prisma Accelerate to connect — it no longer bundles a binary query engine that connects directly via a URL string.

**Fix — `src/lib/prisma.ts`:**
- Installed `@prisma/adapter-pg` and `pg` (the official PostgreSQL driver adapter for Prisma 7)
- `PrismaPg` wraps a connection pool and passes it into `PrismaClient` as `{ adapter }` — this is what the `"client"` engine needs instead of a raw `DATABASE_URL`

**Schema stays unchanged** — in Prisma 7, the `url` lives only in `prisma.config.ts` (already set), not in `schema.prisma`. The `driverAdapters` preview feature is also gone (it graduated to stable in v7).

content.text:
Clean. Here's what changed and why each decision was made:

**`layout.tsx`**
- `Geist` → `Inter` with `variable: "--font-sans"` — this directly sets the `--font-sans` CSS variable that Tailwind v4's `font-sans` utility reads from, so Inter becomes the app-wide default with no extra wiring needed.

**`globals.css`**
- `font-size: 17px` on `html` — every Tailwind rem-based class (`text-sm`, `text-base`, `text-2xl`, etc.) scales proportionally. `text-sm` goes 14px → ~15px, `text-2xl` goes 24px → ~25.5px. Using `17px` on the root is the cleanest global lever since it scales everything consistently.
- `-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased` — ensures Inter renders crisply on macOS/Chrome (subpixel smoothing looks too heavy with Inter's design).

**Auth pages (signup + login)**
- Card title: `font-bold tracking-tight` — Inter's bold weight at this size is sharp; tight tracking prevents the title from feeling loose.
- Card description: `font-medium` — Inter at normal weight reads thin at secondary sizes; medium gives it just enough presence without competing with the title.
- Labels: `font-semibold` — labels above form fields should be clearly legible; `font-medium` from the base component was borderline with Inter at small sizes.
- Footer link: `font-semibold` → stands out clearly against the surrounding muted text without being heavy.

content.text:
Clean. Here's a summary of every change made:

**Password toggle** — both pages
- Added `showPassword` state
- Password `<Input>` gets `type={showPassword ? "text" : "password"}` and `pr-10` (right padding to make room for the icon)
- A plain `<button type="button">` sits `absolute right-2.5 top-1/2 -translate-y-1/2` inside a `relative` wrapper — it renders `<Eye>` or `<EyeOff>` from lucide-react and toggles on click
- `type="button"` is critical — without it the button would submit the form

**Cursor styles**
- Submit button: `cursor-pointer disabled:pointer-events-auto disabled:cursor-not-allowed disabled:opacity-50`. The CVA base class has `disabled:pointer-events-none` which would hide the cursor — `disabled:pointer-events-auto` overrides it via tailwind-merge so `cursor-not-allowed` actually renders
- Toggle button: `cursor-pointer` directly
- Links: `cursor-pointer` added (browsers default to pointer on `<a>`, but explicit is better)

**`FormEvent` deprecation fixed** — both pages now import `type FormEvent` directly from `"react"` instead of using `React.FormEvent` (the deprecated namespace access pattern in React 19 types)

85	> **Good to know**: Other supported HTTP methods are **not** cached, even if they are placed alongside a `GET` method that is cached, in the same file.
86	
87	#### With Cache Components
88	
89	When [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/caching) is enabled, `GET` Route Handlers follow the same model as normal UI routes in your application. They run at request time by default, can be prerendered when they don't access uncached or runtime data, and you can use `use cache` to include uncached data in the static response.
90	
91	**Static example** - doesn't access uncached or runtime data, so it will be prerendered at build time:
92	
93	```tsx filename="app/api/project-info/route.ts"
94	export async function GET() {
95	  return Response.json({
96	    projectName: 'Next.js',
97	  })
98	}
99	```
100	
101	**Dynamic example** - accesses non-deterministic operations. During the build, prerendering stops when `Math.random()` is called, deferring to request-time rendering:
102	
103	```tsx filename="app/api/random-number/route.ts"
104	export async function GET() {
105	  return Response.json({
106	    randomNumber: Math.random(),
107	  })
108	}
109	```
110	
111	**Runtime data example** - accesses request-specific data. Prerendering terminates when runtime APIs like `headers()` are called:

content.text:
Clean. Here's how each security rule is enforced and why each design decision was made:

---

**`GET /api/bookmarks`** — fetches all bookmarks for the session user, ordered newest first. No filter needed beyond `userId: user.id` since Prisma only returns what you query.

**`POST /api/bookmarks`** — `userId` is hardcoded to `user.id` from the session. Even if the request body included a `userId` field it is destructured and discarded. Both `title` and `url` are trimmed before the empty check so `"   "` is also rejected.

**`PATCH /api/bookmarks/[id]`** — ownership is checked *before* parsing the body, so a 403 short-circuits without wasting work. Only the three mutable fields (`title`, `url`, `isPublic`) are extracted — any extra body fields are ignored. Empty-string values for `title`/`url` are rejected even in partial updates. Returns 400 if the body has no recognizable fields at all.

**`DELETE /api/bookmarks/[id]`** — same ownership check pattern. Returns `null` body with `200` (rather than `204`) so fetch callers don't have to special-case an empty response.

**`getSupabaseClient`** — `setAll` is a no-op because the middleware already runs `supabase.auth.getUser()` on every request and refreshes the session token there. Duplicating cookie-setting here would be redundant and could cause a double-set in edge cases.

**Params** — typed as `Promise<{ id: string }>` and `await`-ed, matching the Next.js 16 async params convention shown in the docs.

src/app/dashboard/page.tsx (server component):

- Fetch initial bookmarks by calling GET /api/bookmarks
- Get user handle from Prisma profile WHERE userId = current user id
- Pass initial bookmarks and handle to bookmark-list client component

bookmark-list.tsx (client component):
- Accept initialBookmarks as prop, store in useState
- Show "Add Bookmark" button that opens add modal
- Render bookmark-card for each bookmark
- After POST: append new bookmark to state array
- After PATCH: find bookmark by id in state and replace it
- After DELETE: filter out deleted bookmark from state by id
- No full page refresh, all updates must be instant via state

bookmark-card.tsx:
- Show title, URL as clickable link (opens in new tab)
- Show public/private badge using shadcn Badge
- Edit button opens bookmark-modal with existing values prefilled
- Delete button shows shadcn AlertDialog confirmation before deleting
- Call PATCH /api/bookmarks/[id] for edit
- Call DELETE /api/bookmarks/[id] for delete
- On success call onEdit and onDelete callbacks passed from parent

bookmark-modal.tsx:
- Accept mode (add or edit) and existing bookmark data as props
- Fields: title (required), url (required), isPublic toggle (default false)
- On add: POST to /api/bookmarks, on success call onAdd callback
- On edit: PATCH to /api/bookmarks/[id], on success call onEdit callback
- Show loading state on submit button

UI requirements:
- Clean minimal light design, white background, no dark colors
- cursor-pointer on all clickable elements
- Empty state message when no bookmarks exist: "No bookmarks yet. Add your first one!"
- Use lucide react for icons and shadcn for components like Card, Button etc

content.text:
Zero errors. Here's a breakdown of every key design decision:

---

**`dashboard/page.tsx` (Server Component)**
- `Promise.all` fetches bookmarks and profile in parallel — one round-trip instead of two sequential queries
- Prisma `Date` → ISO string conversion happens at the server/client boundary before passing props (Next.js JSON-serializes props and `Date` becomes `{}` otherwise)
- Logout is a **server action** (`"use server"` inline function) — called via `<form action={logout}>` so it works without a client component; `redirect()` inside a server action triggers a proper 302 redirect

**`bookmark-list.tsx` (Client Component)**
- Exports the `Bookmark` type — used as a single source of truth; card and modal import it with `import type` to avoid circular runtime dependencies
- `handle` is accepted as a prop (passed from server per the spec) but the per-page display of `@handle` lives in the server-rendered navbar to avoid duplication
- All three state mutations (`handleAdd`, `handleEdit`, `handleDelete`) are pure array operations — no network calls, no refetching

**`bookmark-card.tsx`**
- Edit and delete each use local `open` state; the `BookmarkModal` and `AlertDialog` are co-located with the card, rendered via portals so they don't affect layout
- `AlertDialogAction` from base-ui is just a `Button` (no auto-close) — `setDeleteOpen(false)` in the `finally` block handles closing in both success and error paths
- `AlertDialogCancel` wraps `AlertDialogPrimitive.Close` so the Cancel button closes the dialog automatically with no extra handler needed

**`bookmark-modal.tsx`**
- `useEffect` runs when `open` changes to `true` — resets fields for add mode, prefills for edit mode; this means reopening "add" always starts fresh and reopening "edit" always shows the latest saved values
- Discriminated union (`AddProps | EditProps`) gives TypeScript narrowing so `props.bookmark` is only accessed in edit mode and `props.onAdd` only in add mode — no `as` casts needed for `props.bookmark.id`
- `onAdd`/`onEdit` callbacks are invoked with the raw API response (already the updated `Bookmark` shape) — the parent closes the modal immediately after, so no double state update
